skill.name: webapp-testing
skill.description: Use Playwright scripts to inspect, exercise, and debug local web applications while optionally managing development servers.
skill.summary: Test local web applications with Playwright and helper-managed development servers.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-severity hits, but the weak cryptography and Apache URL hits in LICENSE.txt are false positives from license text. The confirmed risk is intentional local command execution in scripts/with_server.py, including shell=True for user-supplied server commands. No evidence found of credential exfiltration, remote beaconing, or a prompt override attempt, so the skill is publishable with a command-execution warning.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Based Local Server Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The helper starts user-provided server commands with shell=True. This is useful for local development workflows, but it can execute arbitrary shell syntax if untrusted command text is supplied.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess.Popen call uses shell=True on the --server argument. The behavior is intentional, but it creates real command-execution risk when command strings are not trusted.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Follow-On Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The helper runs the trailing command supplied by the caller after servers become ready. This is expected for test automation, but users must avoid passing commands from untrusted sources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is parsed from CLI remainder arguments and executed with subprocess.run. It does not use shell=True, which lowers injection risk, but it still executes arbitrary local programs.

medium_findings.title: Instruction Discourages Source Review Before Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents not to read helper source until after trying the script. This can reduce operator visibility before execution, although no explicit security-analysis bypass or authority claim was found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text directly discourages source inspection before running a bundled helper. It appears motivated by context management, so the concern is moderate rather than evidence of malicious prompt injection.

low_findings.title: License Text Scanner False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and Apache URL findings in LICENSE.txt are license boilerplate, not executable code. They do not create cryptographic or network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are standard Apache License text and URLs. No executable cryptographic implementation or runtime network call exists in those locations.

low_findings.title: Localhost and Temporary Screenshot Usage
low_findings.description: The examples use a localhost URL and a /tmp screenshot path for local inspection. This is normal test workflow, but screenshots can contain sensitive UI data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicitly local and does not show exfiltration. The residual concern is limited to temporary storage of captured page content.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen With shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The server helper uses shell=True to support compound development commands. This pattern is dangerous if an attacker can influence the command string.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly passes shell=True to subprocess.Popen. The risk is contextual because the command is caller supplied for local development.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run On Caller-Supplied Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper executes a caller-supplied automation command after server readiness checks. This should only be used with trusted local commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command comes from argparse remainder arguments and is executed directly. Lack of shell=True limits shell injection, but arbitrary program execution remains intended behavior.

content.user_title: Test Local Web Apps with Playwright
content.value_statement: Local web app testing often needs browser automation plus reliable server startup. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Playwright-based inspection, screenshots, console checks, and server-managed test runs.
actual_capabilities: Guides agents to write Python Playwright scripts for local web applications.
Provides a helper script that starts one or more development servers before tests run.
Recommends rendered DOM inspection before choosing selectors for dynamic pages.
Shows how to capture screenshots and browser page content for debugging.
Documents waits for network idle and selectors to reduce flaky automation.
Supports local static HTML and dynamic app testing workflows.
limitations: It does not include a full test framework or assertion library.
It assumes Python Playwright is installed in the working environment.
The server helper executes local commands supplied by the caller.
It focuses on local applications, not remote production monitoring.

use_cases.title: Debug a Frontend Interaction
use_cases.description: Inspect rendered DOM state, choose stable selectors, and reproduce a user action in a browser.

use_cases.title: Run Local UI Smoke Checks
use_cases.description: Start a development server, wait for readiness, and run a browser automation script.

use_cases.title: Capture Evidence for a Bug
use_cases.description: Take screenshots and collect browser observations from a local page before making a fix.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Local Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to inspect my local web page with Playwright. Capture the rendered state and identify useful selectors.

prompt_templates.title: Test a UI Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to write a Playwright script that opens my local app, waits for the page, and tests the main user flow.

prompt_templates.title: Run With a Development Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to start my development server, wait for the configured port, and run a Playwright smoke test.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Dynamic App Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to investigate a failing dynamic UI. Inspect rendered DOM, capture screenshots, check console behavior, and recommend stable selectors.

output_examples.input: Inspect a local React page that does not show buttons until data loads.
output: A screenshot location for the rendered page.
A list of visible buttons and recommended selectors.

output_examples.input: Run a smoke test against an app served on port 5173.
output: A server startup command using the helper.
A browser test outline for the critical path.
A summary of pass, fail, and observed console messages.

output_examples.input: Debug why a login modal cannot be clicked in a local app.
output: Suggested waits and interaction steps to verify the fix.

best_practices: Run helper scripts with --help before using them in a workflow.
Use trusted local server commands because the helper executes them directly.
Wait for network idle or a specific selector before inspecting dynamic pages.
anti_patterns: Do not pass untrusted text into the server command argument.
Do not inspect a dynamic page before waiting for the rendered state.
Do not leave screenshots with sensitive UI data in shared temporary folders.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents test and debug local web applications with Python Playwright scripts.

faq.question: Does it start development servers?
faq.answer: Yes. The included helper can start one or more local servers before running an automation command.

faq.question: Is this safe for untrusted commands?
faq.answer: No. The server helper executes caller-supplied commands and should only use trusted local command text.

faq.question: Does it work with static HTML files?
faq.answer: Yes. The instructions include a path for static HTML inspection and automation.

faq.question: Does it include ready-made test suites?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and a server helper, not a complete test suite.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
